Context: Florida-based founder operating in Florida must choose between incorporating in Delaware or Florida. This decision affects future fundraising, tax treatment, and operational complexity.
Options: Delaware C-Corp with Florida foreign qualification: standard VC-preferred path, predictable corporate law, higher annual cost (~$400 Delaware franchise tax + Florida fees). Florida C-Corp: lower cost, simpler initially, but most institutional investors require Delaware conversion later which triggers tax events and legal fees ($15-30k typical).
Recommendation: Delaware C-Corp with foreign qualification in Florida. The conversion cost later is materially higher than the incremental cost now. Every institutional investor expects Delaware.

Context: Sole founder situation still requires vesting from the company's perspective. Even without co-founders, vesting protects against future scenarios — bringing on co-founders later, investor expectations, acquisition diligence.
Options: Standard 4-year vest 1-year cliff: industry default expected by investors. 4-year vest no cliff: slightly founder-favorable. No vesting: creates fundraising friction and acquisition risk. Accelerated vesting on certain triggers (single trigger or double trigger acceleration on acquisition).
Recommendation: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff and double-trigger acceleration on acquisition. This is the institutional default and provides protection without unusual terms that draw negative attention.

Context: Software trademarks typically file in Class 9 (downloadable software) and Class 42 (SaaS/cloud services). International filing through Madrid Protocol is available but expensive.
Options: US only Class 9 only: minimum coverage ~$350. US Class 9 + 42: standard SaaS coverage ~$700. US + Madrid Protocol key markets (EU UK Canada): broader protection ~$3-5k. Defer all filing until product is closer to launch.
Recommendation: File US in both Class 9 and Class 42 after clearance search. Defer international until material foreign revenue is anticipated. ITU filing allows locking the date before commercial use.
Linked Initiatives: Conduct Trademark Clearance Search,File Trademark Application (Intent to Use)
Open Questions: Will the planned product name survive trademark scrutiny?

Context: Decision affects brand visibility, competitor exposure, SEO benefit, and trademark strategy. Tied to product readiness and trademark clearance.
Options: Stealth until product is fully ready: minimum competitive exposure, slower brand building. Quiet waitlist page now: starts SEO and email list without exposing product. Full public launch announcement at v1: maximum impact but requires product polish and PR.
Recommendation: Quiet waitlist page first. Holding domain content to a simple landing page protects optionality while starting list-building. Avoid broad announcements until product story onboarding and trademark clearance are complete.

Context: Once entity exists, the founder is technically an employee. Compensation level affects runway math, tax treatment, and investor optics during diligence. Zero salary creates explanation burden later.
Options: Zero salary until first financing: maximizes runway but creates diligence question later. Below-market deferred salary (e.g., $1/year or $1k/month accruing): preserves cash with documented intent. Modest market-rate salary ($60-100k): clean diligence story but burns cash. Full market salary: not appropriate pre-revenue.
Recommendation: Below-market documented salary ($1k-3k/month) with founder note retained in books. Creates clean diligence story (founder did not work for free) without material cash burn. Adjust upward at first priced round.

Context: Option pool dilutes founders before any external investment. Sized too small and you re-up before Series A (more dilution). Sized too large and you give up unnecessary equity.
Options: 10% pool: minimal, requires early refresh. 15% pool: standard for pre-seed/seed companies, typical recommendation. 20% pool: aggressive for early stage, justified only if multiple senior hires planned within 12 months.
Recommendation: 15% pool, adoptable when first equity hire is identified. Do not adopt the plan until needed — it just creates unused administrative overhead.

Context: Once first hire or paid contractor exists, need a system for tax compliance, payments, and reporting. Florida has no state income tax which simplifies in-state hiring but out-of-state hires create nexus issues.
Options: Gusto: most popular startup payroll easy setup. Justworks: includes PEO benefits good for first hire wanting health insurance. Deel/Remote: best for international contractors. Manual/CPA-managed: cheapest but error-prone.
Recommendation: Gusto when first US employee is hired. Deel if first paid contributor is international. Defer setup until needed — both platforms onboard in days.
